title: Chapter 9
text: Chapter Six R onin led Lara along the road to the Market. He knew she followed by the sound of her footfalls on the gravel, the squishing when she put weight on her damaged boot, the little noises she made when her feet slipped or she stepped in a particularly deep puddle . Despite that evidence, part of him wanted to look back, just to make sure she was there . Lara’s agreement to his offer was logical; it provided her security and comfort she’d probably never known. With her added stipulation that he assist in the search for her missing sister, she stood only to gain from the arrangement. It was a strange turn — humans, in his experience, were rarely rational in their decisions . Their roles, clearly, were reversed in this case. There had been no logic behind his offer. Only curiosity, an inexplicable need to know what set her apart from everyone else . “So, where are we going?” Lara asked . “You already know.” Ronin turned his head to the right, where the lights of the Bot District shone in contrast to the dark sky . “I know we’re going there . But where in there is your… residence ? Who the hell uses words like that, anyway ?” “I do,” he replied, glancing at her over his shoulder, “and my residence is in the northwest corner of the district. Not far from the Market .” There was something refreshing about conversing with her. Though no two runs in the Dust were ever the same, they were predictable in their own ways — the same prevalent dangers, the same volatile weather, the same reminders of a lost world. And, always, the sense he was on the verge of discerning his true programming . But he couldn’t predict what Lara would say or do from moment to moment. He could learn the signs of a coming outburst, perhaps, but it was impossible to know what would come out of her mouth. Back in the shack, she’d been a few hundredths of a second away from slapping him across the face. It had sent a strange, not unpleasant pulse through him . “So, uh…we gonna run into any, uh…any gearheads ?” The trepidation in her tone and the soft uncertainty of her voice made Ronin frown . “Gearheads?” He searched his memory for the term. If he knew it, it was lost in the Black Out, like so much else. Was it just another slur for his kind? “Of course there will be bots .” “Not just bots . Them .” “You’ll have to be more specific, Lara Brooks .” She muttered, too low for him to make out . “What?” He slowed and twisted to look at her. Her brows were low, lips pressed tightly closed . “Warlord’s cronies!” she spat. “The ones that have his mark .” A skull, fashioned in the shape of a gear. Understanding clicked into place. Now, he had another thing to call Warlord’s bots. Had anyone ever called them gearhead directly? They’d likely be just as confused as Ronin had been . “Yes. Most of the bots in Cheyenne don’t wear his symbol, but he always keeps the ones that do guarding the wall .” Lara frowned, jaw muscles ticking, and stared at the ground in front of her. She hooked her thumbs under the straps of her pack and adjusted its position on her shoulders . “What’s wrong?” he asked. Human expressions could be so telling, but Ronin wasn’t well-versed enough to distinguish their many nuances . “ Nothing .” Her posture belied her answer — the rigidity of her bent arms, her shuffling walk, her head tilted down . “Speak plainly, Lara Brooks. Are you concerned about Warlord’s gearheads ?” “Damn it,” she said, swinging her foot to kick a rock aside, “I don’t want to talk about it .” The rock clattered away, bouncing over the gravel before hitting an exposed railroad tie with a hollow thunk . Ronin halted. Startled, Lara nearly walked into him. He turned, and she looked up at him, squinting against the rain. Moisture clung to the lashes framing her bright blue eyes .” “Should I expect trouble?” he asked . “I don’t know .” “I need to know .” “And I don’t know. You’re a bot, you see his cronies in the Market all the time .” “I’ve never been to the Market with you. There a reason they’d look at you different than anyone else ?” “They don’t like humans.” She dropped her gaze . The rain filled the silence between them. Ronin’s processors would melt in trying to figure this woman out. Her reaction to him, when she’d first realized he was a bot, her adamant refusal to trade with him because of what he was, this aversion to Warlord’s enforcers … “What did they do to you , Lara ?” She was close enough that a slight move of his arm could settle his hand on her hip. How would she respond to such contact? Why did he want it? She’d suffered, somehow, and something hidden deep in his coding said to touch her, to hold her, until she felt better . “They didn’t do anything to me.” She lifted her chin and met his optics. “There’s nothing to talk about. We going, or what ?” Ronin curled his fingers into a fist and let it hang, useless, at his side. No touching; that was one of her conditions. Perhaps he’d been hasty in agreeing to it so easily. She was hiding something, but what could he do about it? Watch her dance, and provide for her. That was the deal . He resumed his walk, leading her through the gates into the Market. They’d used the large, heavy steel doors of a shipping container for the entrance, and Ronin had yet to see them closed . They moved past the Scrapper’s and half a dozen vendor stands, Lara no more than a single stride behind him. She said nothing, and that was concerning. The woman hadn’t let up her protests for more than ten seconds at a time when he carried her back to her dwelling, and her silence now — paired with her obvious anxiety —couldn’t be good . The gap between them increased only when they passed the food vendor. Ronin glanced over his shoulder to see Lara stopped, staring at the food, the tip of her tongue touching her upper lip. Was she still hungry? He recalled the way she’d eaten the dried meat, though eat didn’t seem a strong enough term . There was no food in his dwelling, and that was amongst the most basic of her needs. How had he failed to consider this matter sooner ? He stopped, turning his head to survey the stand’s selection . “I don’t have any food in my residence. Select some to bring .” “But…this stuff costs credits,” she said, not taking her eyes off the bot working the stand. The red-and-white bot’s head was elongated to mimic some sort of hat. It was slicing a carrot, knife rising and falling quickly enough to sound like a distant machine gun firing . Crates of produce filled most of the counter; carrots, cabbages, potatoes, and onions were the only ones Ronin could easily identify. What good did it do him to know what food was called, when he couldn’t eat any of it? More dried meat hung to one side, over what he assumed were smoked cuts of pronghorn and goat. Behind the cook, two pots steamed on a stovetop . The bot stepped toward Lara. “Good evening,” it said pleasantly, “my name is Greene. How may I assist you?” The blank space where the bot’s mouth would have been lit up subtly as it spoke . “That a nickname?” Lara asked . The bot’s optics dilated. “I do not understand your query .” “You’re white .” “No. I am Greene .” “There’s nothing green about you .” The vendor’s optics adjusted again, shutters twisting closed and then opening slowly . “Good evening. My name is Greene. How may I assist you ?” Lara stared at the bot for just over fourteen seconds before turning to look at Ronin. Her brows were raised, jaw slack. She’d clearly not dealt with AIs of Greene’s class before . “Just order some food,” Ronin said, keeping a smile from his lips . “ Anything ?” He pulled his rifle up more securely on his shoulder and dipped a hand into his pocket, drawing out a few chits . “Enough to get you through tonight and tomorrow morning .” Her eyes widened at sight of the credits. She turned back to Greene and pointed to one of the smoked meats. “That. And some of those potatoes and carrots .” “That all?” Ronin asked. He didn’t know how much humans needed to eat and drink, though he was certain the information was somewhere within the smoldering ruins of his memory . “More than I’ve had in weeks,” she muttered. “Gimme some more of that jerky , too .” Greene deftly chopped the vegetables and slid them onto the flat surface of the grill. While they cooked, he cut several slices of the smoked meat and plucked down a few strips of jerky, wrapping both items in their own paper packages . Greene didn’t seem capable of reason or thought on the same level as more advanced bots or synths, but he performed his tasks with efficiency and speed that could only be the result of fulfilled programming. This was Greene’s purpose, the reason he’d been shaped by the Creators and awoken by the Prophet . Ronin settled the payment after Lara received her order, and together they continued their journey through the Market. His olfactory sensors focused on the smell of her food, on the spices the meat had been treated with and the aroma of the cooked vegetables. Scents he’d detected many times, in many places, but, somehow, had never given any consideration. For humans, those smells meant survival . “Why was that one so confused?” she asked, hurrying to walk beside Ronin. She clutched the bundles of food to her chest . “ Greene ?” “ Yeah .” “We’re all shaped by the Creators, but not equally.” He swept the area with his optics, over bots of at least a dozen different models. The synths were all similar, in that they appeared human on the surface, but no two were truly alike beneath . “What do you mean? I know you all look different , but …” “Greene’s function is to prepare food. Other bots are meant only to do things like inspect and repair buildings, or manufacture machinery. Many of us were created with a very specific purpose in mind, and some of us have managed to retain that purpose through the Black Out .” “ Black Out ?” “It happened a long time ago. Long enough that neither of our kinds remembers just what it was. We just…woke up, knowing that some part of us had been lost and that nothing seemed right .” He replayed those earliest memories, the snowy flicker in his optics, the feedback overwhelming his audio receptors. Nothing remained of who he had been before. There was only static, the slow churn of diagnostics and reboots, and the kindly voice of the one who’d reactivated him . Lara wore a troubled frown, eyes shifting back and forth over the ground at her feet. Didn’t humans tell any stories of those times? They had to; all the Creator’s children had experienced the Black Out. Even if their lives were relatively short, wouldn’t they have passed the knowledge down from generation to generation ? “What’s your purpose?” she asked . For an instant, Ronin’s processors stilled, and everything in him was quiet . Good evening. My name is Ronin, because I cannot remember what it used to be. How may I assist you ? What answer could he possibly give? He’d searched after it for nearly two centuries and still had no satisfying answer . They reached the gate into the Bot District. This one was also open, but only wide enough to allow people through single-file. Large flood lights atop the wall bathed the area in harsh white light . The timing of their arrival was convenient . “No more conversation. I’ll talk if they ask anything,” he said . Two gearheads stood guard, one on each side of the gap. Their weapons were more formidable than those of the bots at the roadblock on the edge of town. These were pre-Black Out assault rifles, built with matching parts. Even without armor-piercing rounds, they had a chance of penetrating Ronin’s casing from this range . Lara fell behind Ronin, following close on his heels. The gearheads’ optics flickered from her to Ronin. Perhaps he should have had the foresight to remove his own rifle from its protective carrier before coming here . “Dustwalker,” one of the gearheads — a synth — said in greeting. The right sleeve of his coat had been removed, displaying the bare blue casing of his arm, and Warlord’s mark on his shoulder . “Cobalt.” Ronin nodded. He slowed to a stop several feet away from the gearheads. Lara didn’t bump into him this time, but pressed lightly against his back. His skin flared to life, electrodes firing off a wave of pleasure at her touch despite the clothing separating them . “Heard you had a chat with Warlord last night,” the other bot said. It was a squat model with a battered, blocky casing. Went by the name Northside . Lara stiffened and inhaled sharply . They didn’t do anything to me . Why did she react like that at mention of Ronin meeting Warlord ? “ I did .” “Convinced you to sample some local goods?” asked Cobalt . Heat pulsed off Lara. She shifted, as though to move around him; simulations suggested she was likely to go into one of her outbursts . Ronin reached back, placing a hand on her hip, and glanced back at her over his shoulder. She stared at him with jaw clenched and eyes ablaze, but didn’t move . “There is an agreement in place,” he said, loud enough for the gearheads to hear . She eased back, tension draining from her body. Volatile things, humans. But hadn’t Ronin reacted in similar fashion to Warlord and his gearheads ? “Go on in with her, then. Creators know I’d be needing something like that after being in the Dust as long as you were .” The gearheads stepped aside. Lara fell into step immediately behind Ronin as he walked. They were almost there; through the gates and less than half a mile to his residence . “Send word when you’re done with her,” Cobalt said. “I wouldn’t mind a go. Not many of them have hair like that .” A twitch skittered over the palm of Ronin’s hand, fingers curling as though to grip his rifle. How many gearheads served Warlord? Ronin needed to know how many bullets to stockpile . He led Lara through the narrow opening, crossing the threshold from her world into what should have been his . Ahead of them, the broad street — lined by orange streetlights — curved away to the southeast. Directly north lay the park, grass and leaves glistening with moisture in the artificial light. It was one of the greenest places Ronin had seen since waking. Yet there’d never been anyone save the maintenance bots within, trimming the vegetation . It seemed a waste . There were only a few brown patches in the grass, and it thrived in many spots thanks to the shade of the trees ringing the park. He could just make out the water of the central pond through the trunks, reflecting the glow of Cheyenne’s lights on the clouds . “They are not touching me,” Lara hissed from behind. “And what the hell did you and Warlord talk about ?” Ronin turned to face her, scanning the empty street and the imposing, eclectic wall. Though none were in sight, there were gearheads all over Cheyenne, and they all reported faithfully to their leader . “This way. Shouldn’t be talking about that out here .” “I’m not moving another step.” She folded her arms across her chest. “Tell me. Why did you have a chat with that…that thing ?” She’d made her loathing of bots clear when Ronin first met her, but this was somehow different. Though she didn’t raise her voice, venom dripped from every word . Here, in the middle of the main road leading to the heart of the Bot District. Here, where gearheads were likely to walk by at any moment . Ronin stepped close to her, tilting his head down to meet her gaze. Every trip into the Dust put him in danger, but he had no desire to tempt deactivation here , now . “This is not the place to speak of these things, Lara Brooks .” “Well, I’m not going with you. Food be damned. I won’t be a part of any plans that involve that thing .” She turned and walked back toward the gate . “I’m not going to follow you back through. When they see you alone …” “I’d prefer a bullet in the back .” “We both know it won’t be a bullet .” Lara froze. She hung her head, water streaming from her wrap and the stray strands of her hair. Her free hand trembled in a fist at her side. Whatever wounds she’d suffered were still fresh . He closed the distance between them. She didn’t move away . “I’ll tell you what you want to know as soon as we’re secure in my residence,” he said, softening his voice . She turned her head, angling her ear toward him . “Warlord is not involved in this, I can assure you. And I don’t want him to be any more than you do .” Her shoulders rose with a deep inhalation. A moment later, she released a slow, shuddering breath and opened her hand. The red spots on her fingers and palm faded away . “Come,” Ronin said .
